peer_exemplars.reasoning: High populist people-centrism and participatory democracy preference drive moderate support, but low openness and high authoritarian submission temper enthusiasm for unstructured citizen-led processes.
Low openness and high authoritarianism lead to skepticism of participatory democracy, but moderate libertarianism tempers opposition.

Low agreeableness and high extraversion combined with strong populist people-centrism (59.68) and very low deference to authority (0.49) make this person skeptical of elite-driven deliberative bodies, but openness to new ideas tempers outright rejection.

High authoritarianism and low openness to experience, combined with a strong preference for strong leaders and distrust of participatory democracy, lead to mild opposition to citizen assemblies as they are seen as inefficient and potentially disruptive to established order.

High openness and participatory democracy preference (60.78) drive moderate support for citizen assemblies, but low trust in institutions tempers enthusiasm.
High openness and extraversion support participatory democracy, but low agreeableness and high anti-elitism temper enthusiasm for deliberative bodies.

High authoritarianism (94.28) and low GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference (28.17) drive opposition to citizen assemblies as they threaten elite-led order, though high agreeableness (72.94) tempers the rejection.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong conventionalism and authoritarian submission support accepting electoral outcomes, though high anti-elitism and low trust in institutions create some skepticism about fairness.
High system justification and moderate political trust support acceptance of electoral outcomes despite low agreeableness.

High socio-cultural liberalism (93.18) and strong fairness/equality moral foundation (73.59) support democratic norms, but low conscientiousness and moderate political trust (69.83) prevent maximal endorsement.

High conscientiousness, strong system justification, and high trust in institutions, along with a high value on order and stability, produce clear support for the norm of loser consent as essential for democratic stability and peaceful transitions.

Strong support for democratic norms due to high trust in institutions and low collective narcissism, but moderate system justification tempers extreme endorsement.

Strong system justification (84.95) and populist people-centrism (88.02) support the norm of loser consent to maintain institutional legitimacy, but high authoritarianism (94.28) slightly reduces enthusiasm for full opposition rights.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High libertarian skepticism of state control and free-spirit autonomy orientation create mild opposition to mandated participation, despite authoritarian tendencies.

Strong libertarian skepticism of state control (46.35) combined with very low authoritarian submission (22.23) and high extraversion-driven desire for autonomy make compulsory voting feel coercive.

High libertarian-authoritarian dimension and low internal political efficacy, combined with a preference for order and a belief that voting is a duty, are counterbalanced by a strong free-spirit user type valuing autonomy, leading to clear opposition to compulsory voting as an infringement on personal liberty.
Moderate libertarian civil liberties support (59.85) and a preference for autonomy (free spirit autonomy 66.15) create clear opposition to mandatory voting as an infringement on personal freedom.
Libertarian anti-authoritarian leanings (74.11) and high autonomy (87.87) oppose compulsory voting, yet high civic nationalism (60.98) and people-centrism (65.15) pull toward mild support.
Libertarian leanings and low authoritarianism oppose mandatory voting, but high civic engagement and internal efficacy create mild ambivalence.

Low libertarianism (30.64) and high authoritarianism (94.28) might seem to support compulsion, but low internal political efficacy (31.65) and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style make forced participation feel coercive and ineffective.

High openness and low authoritarianism favor proportional representation, but strong national identity and preference for stability temper support.

Low GAL-TAN participatory democracy (28.17) and high authoritarianism (94.28) favor majoritarian systems for stability, but high populist people-centrism (88.02) creates mild openness to proportionality, resulting in near-neutrality.

Moderate trust in civil service, supported by system justification but limited by low agreeableness.
Low agreeableness (34.24) and moderate political trust (69.83) combined with personal experience of bureaucratic barriers produce only mild trust.

High trust in institutions and a belief in the competence of experts, combined with high conscientiousness and a value on order and efficiency, produce clear trust in the civil service as a professional, non-partisan body that implements policy effectively and maintains the continuity of the state.
High general political trust (97.99) and high conscientiousness (59.82) combine with moderate external political efficacy (75.47) to produce clear trust in the civil service.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Mild distrust from low institutional trust and high anti-elitism, though conventionalism and authoritarian submission provide some baseline confidence in electoral procedures.
Moderate trust in electoral process, consistent with system justification and low populist attitudes.
Low political efficacy (20.08) and moderate populist attitudes (54.96) combined with low system justification (27.96) create near-neutral trust, reflecting ambivalence about electoral integrity.

High system justification and a strong belief in the legitimacy of current institutions, combined with high trust in the electoral process and a value on order, produce strong trust in the electoral process as the fundamental mechanism for choosing leaders and legitimizing the system, though this is a pragmatic trust rather than a naive one.
Very high system justification (92.22) and political trust (97.99) combined with high voting participation (94.7) produce strong trust in the electoral process.
Moderate trust in electoral process (51.79) and high internal political efficacy (89.34) support some trust, but high collective narcissism (89.11) and low system justification (31.96) reduce it.
High political trust and system justification support trust, but high populist anti-elitism and low agreeableness create some doubt.

Low trust in electoral process (implied by low political trust mean 40.11) and high populist anti-elitism (78.24) produce distrust in elections, but high system justification (84.95) partially counters this skepticism.
